conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I've got three children actually - Alex who's 4, Blake who's 16, and Charlie who's 15. I'm wondering if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you have three children - Alex (4), Blake (16), and Charlie (15).

Now I need to understand a bit more about each child's situation to assess eligibility properly.

Let me start with Blake, who is 16. Is Blake currently in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake actually left school recently, but they've just signed up with the local careers service to figure out what they want to do next. They're not in education or training at the moment though.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Blake. That's helpful to know they've registered with the careers service.

Now, for Charlie who is 15 - is Charlie currently in education (such as school)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie's still at school - they're in Year 10 doing their GCSEs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. And just to confirm about Alex who is 4 - I assume Alex lives with you?

Now I need to ask you a few general questions:

Do all three children currently live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex and Charlie live with me, yes. But Blake doesn't - they live with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Blake lives with your ex-partner, I need to understand your situation a bit more.

Do you contribute financially towards Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or somewhere to live)? And if so, is that contribution at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment or more?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Blake. I help out with things like clothes, school supplies, and give them some pocket money regularly. I'm pretty sure it's more than what Child Benefit would be - I think I spend around £50 a week on them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful information, thank you. And just to clarify - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you. And just to confirm - are any of your children currently in hospital, care, or residential accommodation for an extended period?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually yes, Alex has been in hospital for a little while now - about three months or so. Nothing too serious thankfully, but they've needed to stay in for treatment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm glad to hear it's nothing too serious, and I hope Alex recovers well. 

Since Alex has been in hospital for about three months, I need to ask: do you regularly spend money on Alex while they're in hospital (for example on toys, treats, clothes, or other items for them)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy things for Alex - you know, little bits to keep them entertained like colouring books, their favourite snacks, some new pyjamas, that sort of thing. I visit regularly and always bring something for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's wonderful that you're supporting Alex during their hospital stay. 

Just one final question: has Blake started any paid work of 24 hours or more per week, or are they claiming any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't working - well, they do a little Saturday job at a café but that's only a few hours. And they're not claiming any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for all three of your children**: Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Here's the breakdown:

**Alex (age 4):** You're eligible despite Alex being in hospital for 3 months. Although Child Benefit normally stops after 12 weeks in hospital, there's an exception when you regularly spend money on the child during their hospital stay. Since you're buying items like colouring books, snacks, and pyjamas for Alex during your visits, you continue to qualify.

**Blake (age 16):** You're eligible for Blake even though they've left education and live with your ex-partner. This is because Blake has registered with the careers service, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks. Additionally, since you contribute around £50 per week towards Blake's upkeep (which is more than the Child Benefit amount), you're considered responsible for Blake under the rules.

**Charlie (age 15):** You're eligible for Charlie as they're under 16, live with you, and are still in school doing their GCSEs.

Since no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these children, you should be able to claim for all three. You can proceed with your Child Benefit application or claim!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old, which is under 16, so they qualify based on age. Although Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (which exceeds the 12-week limit after which Child Benefit normally stops), there is an exception to this rule: you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on the child while they're in hospital. You confirmed that you regularly buy items for Alex such as colouring books, snacks, and pyjamas during your visits. Therefore, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex despite the extended hospital stay.

Blake is 16 years old and has left education. Normally, Child Benefit continues for children aged 16-19 only if they remain in approved education or training. However, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Blake has registered with the local careers service, which qualifies them for this 20-week continuation period. Additionally, although Blake lives with your ex-partner (not with you), you are contributing towards Blake's upkeep with approximately £50 per week for clothes, school supplies, and pocket money, which is more than the Child Benefit amount. This means you are responsible for Blake under the rules. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake during this 20-week period.

Charlie is 15 years old and currently attending school (Year 10, doing GCSEs). As Charlie is under 16 and lives with you, you automatically qualify for Child Benefit for Charlie. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, and Charlie has not started work of 24 hours or more per week or claimed benefits in their own right. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You live in the UK (Leeds) and are responsible for three children. You are eligible for Child Benefit for all three children: Alex (4) qualifies despite being in hospital for 3 months because you regularly spend money on them; Blake (16) qualifies because they registered with the careers service after leaving education (allowing a 20-week continuation period) and you contribute more than the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep despite them living with your ex-partner; and Charlie (15) qualifies as they are under 16, live with you, and are in school. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these children.

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, and Charlie has not started work of 24 hours or more per week or claimed benefits in their own right. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these children.
